types.name: AbstractQueryBuilder
types.javadoc_summary: Base class for fluent SQL builders.

methods.name: sqlDialect
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the SqlDialect this builder renders SQL with.

methods.name: into

params.name: tableName
params.javadoc: the name of the target table (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Specifies the target table for an INSERT or INSERT ... SELECT operation.
contract: Must be called after setting the columns/values via insert(...) or the columns to copy via select(...).
When chained after select(...), the eventual from(...) call appends the source query, producing INSERT INTO target (cols) SELECT cols FROM source.

methods.name: into

params.name: entityClass
params.javadoc: the entity class representing the target table (must not be null)

methods.javadoc_summary: Specifies the target table for an INSERT or INSERT ... SELECT operation using an entity class.
examples:
String sql = PSC.insert(account).into(Account.class).build().query();
// Table name derived from Account class based on naming policy

methods.name: into
methods.signature: public This into(final String tableName, final Class<?> entityClass)

params.name: tableName
params.javadoc: the name of the target table (must not be null, empty, or blank)

params.name: entityClass
params.javadoc: the entity class for property mapping (may be null)

methods.javadoc_summary: Specifies the target table for an INSERT or INSERT ... SELECT operation with an explicit table name and entity class.
examples:
String sql = PSC.insert(account).into("account_archive", Account.class).build().query();
// Inserts into specified table with Account class mapping

methods.name: distinct
methods.javadoc_summary: Adds DISTINCT clause to the SELECT statement.
examples:
String sql = PSC.select("name").distinct().from("account").build().query();
// Output: SELECT DISTINCT name FROM account

methods.name: selectModifier

params.name: selectModifier
params.javadoc: modifiers like ALL, DISTINCT, DISTINCTROW, TOP, etc.; may be null or empty (no-op)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a pre-select modifier to the SELECT statement.
contract: For better performance, this method should be called before from.
examples:
String sql = PSC.select("*").selectModifier("TOP 10").from("account").build().query();
// Output: SELECT TOP 10 * FROM account

methods.name: limit

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the maximum number of rows to return

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a row-count restriction to the query, rendered in the dialect's pagination syntax.
contract: FETCH together with an ORDER BY clause); the OFFSET 0 ROWS prefix is omitted when #offset(int) has already been called any other product, or no product info: LIMIT count On the FETCH-style dialects (Oracle, DB2, SQL Server) this method also consumes the OFFSET and FETCH slots, because OFFSET must precede FETCH: call #offset(int) before this method, or prefer #limit(int, int), which emits the combined clause in the correct order.

methods.name: limit
methods.signature: public This limit(final int count, final int offset)

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the maximum number of rows to return

params.name: offset
params.javadoc: the number of rows to skip

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a count-plus-offset pagination clause, rendered in the dialect's pagination syntax.

methods.name: offset

params.name: offset
params.javadoc: the number of rows to skip

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds an OFFSET clause to skip a number of rows, rendered in the dialect's pagination syntax.
contract: On Oracle, DB2 and SQL Server dialects (per SqlDialect.ProductInfo) the clause is rendered as OFFSET offset ROWS; on those dialects call this method before #limit(int), because OFFSET must precede FETCH.

methods.name: offsetRows

params.name: offset
params.javadoc: the number of rows to skip

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds an OFFSET ROWS clause (SQL:2008 standard syntax).
examples:
String sql = PSC.select("*")
                .from("users")
                .orderBy("id")
                .offsetRows(20)
                .fetchNextRows(10)
                .build().query();
// Output: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY id OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY

methods.name: fetchNextRows

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the number of rows to fetch

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a FETCH NEXT N ROWS ONLY clause (SQL:2008 standard syntax).
contract: Call #offsetRows(int) first when an offset is needed; #offset(int) is also compatible when this builder uses a FETCH-style dialect.
examples:
String sql = PSC.select("*")
                .from("users")
                .orderBy("id")
                .offsetRows(0)
                .fetchNextRows(10)
                .build().query();
// Output: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY id OFFSET 0 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY

methods.name: fetchFirstRows

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the number of rows to fetch

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a FETCH FIRST N ROWS ONLY clause (SQL standard syntax).
contract: Call #offsetRows(int) first when an offset is needed; #offset(int) is also compatible when this builder uses a FETCH-style dialect.

methods.name: append

params.name: condition
params.javadoc: the condition to append

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends a condition to the SQL statement.
contract: method: it must follow a SELECT segment completed by from(...), must precede ORDER BY, pagination, and FOR UPDATE, its operand must be a complete read-only SELECT sub-query, and afterwards only compound-result clauses (ORDER BY, pagination, FOR UPDATE) may follow.

methods.name: append

params.name: expr
params.javadoc: the expression to append

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends a string expression to the SQL statement.
contract: A single separating space is inserted before expr when, and only when, it is needed: that is, when the statement built so far does not already end with a space and expr does not already begin with one.

As a result both .append("FOR UPDATE") and .append(" FOR UPDATE") produce the same, correctly spaced output (a doubled space is possible only when the statement built so far already ends with a space and expr also begins with one).

methods.javadoc_summary: Creates a SELECT statement from a single Selection descriptor.
contract: Prefer it over the positional select(Class, ...) overloads when you need full control (table alias, class alias, sub-entity inclusion, property exclusion) over a single entity, because each attribute is set through a named Selection.SelectionBuilder method rather than by argument position.
examples:
SqlBuilder sql = PSC.select(Selection.builder(Account.class)
        .tableAlias("a")
        .classAlias("account")
        .excludedPropNames(N.asSet("password"))
        .build());

methods.name: select

params.javadoc: list of Selection objects defining what to select from each entity

methods.javadoc_summary: Creates a SELECT statement for multiple entities using Selection descriptors.
examples:
List<Selection> selections = Arrays.asList(
    Selection.builder(Account.class).tableAlias("a").classAlias("account").build(),
    Selection.builder(Order.class).tableAlias("o").classAlias("order")
        .includedPropNames(Arrays.asList("id", "total")).build(),
    Selection.builder(Product.class).tableAlias("p").classAlias("product")
        .excludedPropNames(N.asSet("description")).build()
);

String sql = PSC.select(selections)
                .from("account a")
                .innerJoin("orders o").on("a.id = o.account_id")
                .innerJoin("order_items oi").on("o.id = oi.order_id")
                .innerJoin("products p").on("oi.product_id = p.id")
                .build().query();

types.name: DynamicQuery
types.javadoc_summary: Entry point for fluently creating dynamic SQL queries programmatically.

methods.name: builder
methods.javadoc_summary: Creates a new Builder instance for constructing a dynamic SQL query.

types.name: Builder
types.javadoc_summary: Builder for constructing dynamic SQL queries clause by clause.

methods.name: select
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the SelectClause builder for defining columns to retrieve.

methods.name: from
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the FromClause builder for defining tables and joins.

methods.name: where

methods.name: groupBy
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the GroupByClause builder for defining grouping columns.

methods.name: orderBy

methods.name: limit

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the maximum number of rows to return (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a LIMIT clause to restrict the number of rows returned.

methods.name: limit
methods.signature: public Builder limit(final int count, final int offset)

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the maximum number of rows to return (must not be negative)

params.name: offset
params.javadoc: the number of rows to skip (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a LIMIT clause with count and offset for pagination.
contract: Generates: LIMIT count OFFSET offset; the OFFSET portion is omitted when offset is 0, emitting just LIMIT count.

methods.name: offset

params.name: offset
params.javadoc: the number of rows to skip (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a plain OFFSET clause to skip the given number of leading rows.
contract: When offset is 0, nothing is appended.
Use #offsetRows(int) instead when you need the SQL:2008 OFFSET n ROWS form (typically paired with #fetchNextRows(int) or #fetchFirstRows(int)).
examples:
builder.limit(10).offset(20);
// Generates: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20

methods.name: offsetRows

params.name: offset
params.javadoc: the number of rows to skip (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds an OFFSET clause for SQL:2008 standard pagination.
contract: When offset is 0, nothing is appended.
examples:
builder.offsetRows(20).fetchNextRows(10);
// Generates: OFFSET 20 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY

methods.name: fetchNextRows

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the number of rows to fetch (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a FETCH NEXT clause for SQL:2008 standard result limiting.
examples:
builder.offsetRows(100).fetchNextRows(25);
// Generates: OFFSET 100 ROWS FETCH NEXT 25 ROWS ONLY

methods.name: fetchFirstRows

params.name: count
params.javadoc: the number of rows to fetch (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a FETCH FIRST clause for SQL:2008 standard result limiting.
examples:
builder.fetchFirstRows(10);
// Generates: FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY

methods.name: union

params.name: query
params.javadoc: the complete SQL query to union with (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a UNION operator to combine results with another query.

builder.union("SELECT id, name FROM archived_users");

methods.name: unionAll

params.name: query

methods.name: intersect

params.name: query
params.javadoc: the complete SQL query to intersect with (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds an INTERSECT operator to find common rows between queries.

methods.name: except

params.name: query
params.javadoc: the complete SQL query whose result rows are subtracted from the current result set (must not be null, empty, or blank)

params.name: query
params.javadoc: the complete SQL query whose result rows are subtracted from the current result set (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds a MINUS operator to find rows in the first query but not in the second.

methods.name: append

params.name: textToAppend
params.javadoc: the complete raw SQL clause to append verbatim (e.g., "LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20") (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends a raw, database-specific SQL clause or fragment verbatim to the end of the query.
contract: The supplied text is emitted unchanged (preceded by a separating space when needed; see below) and is not validated, escaped, or interpreted in any way \u2014 whatever you pass becomes the literal tail of the generated SQL.

A single separating space is inserted before textToAppend when, and only when, it is needed: that is, when the text built so far does not already end with a space and textToAppend does not already begin with one.
Consequently, either orderBy().append(...) or a trailing append("ORDER BY ...") can order a combined set-operation result; prefer the typed form when it is sufficient.

methods.name: appendIf
methods.signature: public Builder appendIf(final boolean b, final String textToAppend)

params.name: b
params.javadoc: the condition to check

params.name: textToAppend
params.javadoc: the raw SQL clause to append verbatim if b is true (must not be null, empty, or blank when b is true)

methods.javadoc_summary: Conditionally appends a raw SQL clause or fragment verbatim to the end of the query.
contract: When b is true this behaves exactly like #append(String) (a single separating space is inserted only when needed, then the text is emitted unchanged with no validation, escaping, or interpretation); when b is false the builder is left unchanged and textToAppend is not inspected.
examples:
builder.appendIf(paged, "LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20")
       .appendIf(locked, "FOR UPDATE");

methods.name: appendIfOrElse
methods.signature: public Builder appendIfOrElse(final boolean b, final String textToAppendWhenTrue, final String textToAppendWhenFalse)

params.name: b
params.javadoc: the condition to check

params.name: textToAppendWhenTrue
params.javadoc: the raw SQL clause to append if condition is true (must not be null, empty, or blank)

params.name: textToAppendWhenFalse
params.javadoc: the raw SQL clause to append if condition is false (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends one of two raw SQL clauses verbatim to the end of the query based on a boolean condition.
contract: Always appends something, choosing between the two options; the chosen text is emitted exactly as #append(String) would emit it (a single separating space is inserted only when needed, with no validation, escaping, or interpretation).
examples: builder.appendIfOrElse(smallPage, "LIMIT 10", "LIMIT 100");

methods.name: build
methods.javadoc_summary: Builds the final SQL string from all the components and releases resources.
contract: This method MUST be called to get the SQL and clean up internal resources.
After calling build(), this builder is closed and must not be reused: any subsequent call to a builder method (including build() itself) throws IllegalStateException.

types.name: SelectClause
types.javadoc_summary: Builder class for constructing the SELECT clause of a SQL query.

methods.name: append

params.name: column
params.javadoc: the column name to select (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends a single column to the SELECT clause.
examples:
select.append("user_id").append("username");
// Generates: SELECT user_id, username

methods.name: append
methods.signature: public SelectClause append(final String column, final String alias)

params.name: column
params.javadoc: the column name to select (must not be null, empty, or blank)

params.name: alias
params.javadoc: the alias for the column (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends a column with an alias to the SELECT clause.
examples:
select.append("first_name", "fname").append("last_name", "lname");
// Generates: SELECT first_name AS fname, last_name AS lname

methods.name: append

params.name: columns
params.javadoc: collection of column names to select (may be null or empty; individual elements must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends multiple columns to the SELECT clause.
contract: If the collection is empty, this method does nothing.
A non-empty collection is snapshotted before validation and rendering, so both phases observe the same elements even if the supplied collection is live or mutable.
examples:
select.append(Arrays.asList("id", "name", "email"));
// Generates: SELECT id, name, email

methods.name: append
methods.signature: public SelectClause append(final Map<String, String> columnAliases)

params.name: columnAliases
params.javadoc: map where keys are column names and values are aliases (may be null or empty; individual keys and values must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends multiple columns with their aliases to the SELECT clause.
contract: If the map is empty, this method does nothing.
Columns are emitted in the map's iteration order, so use a java.util.LinkedHashMap if a stable column order matters.

methods.name: appendIf
methods.signature: public SelectClause appendIf(final boolean b, final String textToAppend)

params.name: b
params.javadoc: the condition to check

params.name: textToAppend
params.javadoc: the string to append if condition is true (must not be null, empty, or blank when b is true)

methods.javadoc_summary: Conditionally appends a string to the SELECT clause based on a boolean condition.
contract: The string is only appended if the condition is true.
examples:
select.appendIf(includeSalary, "salary")
      .appendIf(includeBonus, "bonus");

params.name: textToAppendWhenFalse

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends one of two strings to the SELECT clause based on a boolean condition.

methods.name: join

types.name: WhereClause
types.javadoc_summary: Builder class for constructing the WHERE clause of a SQL query.

methods.name: append

params.name: expr
params.javadoc: the SQL expression to append (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends a condition to the WHERE clause.
contract: Unlike #and(String)/#or(String), this method does not insert a logical connective \u2014 the caller must include any required AND/OR in the argument.
examples:
where.append("active = true").append("AND deleted = false");
// Generates: WHERE active = true AND deleted = false

methods.name: appendPlaceholders

params.name: placeholderCount
params.javadoc: the number of question marks to append (must not be negative)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends question mark placeholders for parameterized queries.
contract: Use the #appendPlaceholders(int, String, String) overload when you also need a prefix/postfix (e.g.
Usage Examples: If placeholderCount is 0, nothing is appended.

methods.name: appendPlaceholders
methods.signature: public WhereClause appendPlaceholders(final int placeholderCount, final String prefix, final String postfix)

params.name: placeholderCount
params.javadoc: the number of question marks to append (must not be negative)

params.name: prefix
params.javadoc: the string to add before the question marks (must not be null)

params.name: postfix
params.javadoc: the string to add after the question marks (must not be null)

methods.javadoc_summary: Appends question mark placeholders surrounded by prefix and postfix.
contract: Usage Examples: If placeholderCount is 0, neither prefix nor postfix is appended.
examples:
where.append("status IN ").appendPlaceholders(3, "(", ")");
// Generates: status IN (?, ?, ?)

methods.name: and

params.name: expr
params.javadoc: the SQL expression to add with AND (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds an AND condition to the WHERE clause.
contract: If called before any #append(String), this acts as the first condition and emits WHERE cond with no leading AND.
examples:
where.append("active = true").and("age >= 18").and("country = ?");
// Generates: WHERE active = true AND age >= 18 AND country = ?

methods.name: or

params.name: expr
params.javadoc: the SQL expression to add with OR (must not be null, empty, or blank)

methods.javadoc_summary: Adds an OR condition to the WHERE clause.
contract: If called before any #append(String), this acts as the first condition and emits WHERE cond with no leading OR.
examples:
where.append("role = 'admin'").or("role = 'moderator'");
// Generates: WHERE role = 'admin' OR role = 'moderator'

contract: The string is only appended if the condition is true.
examples:

methods.signature: public static Limit limit(final String expr)

params.name: expr
params.javadoc: the limit expression as a string (must not be null, empty, or blank, and must match one of the accepted forms)

methods.javadoc_summary: Creates a LIMIT clause from a string expression, formatting and validating it against a fixed grammar.
contract: The expression is trimmed, its internal whitespace collapsed, and its SQL keywords upper-cased (parameter names left intact); a "LIMIT " prefix is prepended when it begins with a digit, '?', ':', or "#{" .

It must be one of LIMIT count, LIMIT count OFFSET offset, MySQL's LIMIT offset, count, or the SQL:2008 [OFFSET offset ROWS] FETCH NEXT/FIRST count ROWS ONLY forms \u2014 where each number is an integer or a ?
When the condition is rendered by a SQL builder, a parsed expression is emitted in the target dialect's pagination syntax (so MySQL's comma form and the FETCH forms are re-rendered per dialect).

An opaque (placeholder) expression is re-rendered in the dialect's FETCH syntax only when the dialect paginates with OFFSET/FETCH (Oracle, DB2 or SQL Server, per SqlDialect.ProductInfo) and it is a generic LIMIT count [OFFSET offset] form; otherwise it is emitted verbatim.
examples:
Limit limit = Filters.limit("10 OFFSET 20");
// Renders as: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20

methods.javadoc_summary: Retrieves the SqlOperation enum value corresponding to the given operation name.
examples:
SqlOperation selectOp = SqlOperation.of("SELECT");      // returns SELECT
SqlOperation insertOp = SqlOperation.of("INSERT");      // returns INSERT
SqlOperation mergeOp = SqlOperation.of("MERGE");        // returns MERGE

// Case-insensitive; multi-word and enum constant names are accepted
SqlOperation lower = SqlOperation.of("select");                  // returns SELECT
SqlOperation tx = SqlOperation.of("begin transaction");          // returns BEGIN_TRANSACTION
SqlOperation txByName = SqlOperation.of("BEGIN_TRANSACTION");    // returns BEGIN_TRANSACTION

// Edge cases
SqlOperation unsupported = SqlOperation.of("TRUNCATE");   // returns null (not supported)
SqlOperation blank = SqlOperation.of("");                 // returns null
SqlOperation nil = SqlOperation.of(null);                 // returns null

methods.javadoc_summary: Checks if this Binary condition is equal to another object.
contract: Checks if this Binary condition is equal to another object.
Two conditions are equal only if they are of the exact same runtime class and have the same property name, operator, and value.

The runtime class is part of the equality contract, so an instance of one concrete subclass is never equal to an instance of a different subclass (or to a raw Binary), even when their property name, operator, and value all match.

types.name: Criteria
types.javadoc_summary: A container representing a complete SQL query structure composed of multiple clauses (Join, Where, GroupBy, Having, OrderBy, Limit, and set operations like Union/UnionAll/Intersect/Except/Minus).

methods.name: selectModifier
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the SELECT modifier (e.g., DISTINCT, DISTINCTROW, DISTINCT ON (col1, col2), or any custom modifier set via Builder#selectModifier(String)), or null if none was set.
contract: Returns the SELECT modifier (e.g., DISTINCT, DISTINCTROW, DISTINCT ON (col1, col2), or any custom modifier set via Builder#selectModifier(String)), or null if none was set.
examples:
Criteria.builder().build().selectModifier();                    // returns null
Criteria.builder().distinct().build().selectModifier();         // returns "DISTINCT"
Criteria.builder().distinctOn("a, b").build().selectModifier(); // returns "DISTINCT ON (a, b)"

methods.name: joins
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns all JOIN clauses (JOIN, INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL JOIN, CROSS JOIN, NATURAL JOIN) in the order they were added.
examples:
Criteria.builder().build().joins();   // returns [] (empty list)

Criteria c = Criteria.builder().join("orders").innerJoin("items").build();
c.joins().size();                     // returns 2
c.joins().add(null);                  // throws UnsupportedOperationException (unmodifiable view)

examples:
Criteria.builder().build().conditions();   // returns [] (empty list)

c.conditions().size();                      // returns 2
c.conditions().clear();                     // throws UnsupportedOperationException (unmodifiable view)

methods.name: findConditions

params.name: operator
params.javadoc: the operator to match (may be null, in which case this returns an empty list since AbstractCondition disallows null operators)

methods.javadoc_summary: Returns all conditions whose Condition#operator() equals the given operator, in the order they were added.
examples:
Criteria c = Criteria.builder().join("o1").join("o2").where(Filters.eq("a", 1)).build();
c.findConditions(Operator.JOIN).size();    // returns 2 (JOINs accumulate)
c.findConditions(Operator.WHERE).size();   // returns 1
c.findConditions(Operator.HAVING);         // returns [] (no HAVING present)
c.findConditions(null);                    // returns [] (null never matches an operator)

methods.name: distinct
methods.javadoc_summary: Sets the DISTINCT modifier for the query.
contract: When the criteria is appended to a SqlBuilder, it is applied to that builder's current SELECT segment.

    .distinct()
    .where(Filters.equal("status", "active"))
    .build();
c.selectModifier();                 // returns "DISTINCT"
c.toSql(NamingPolicy.NO_CHANGE);    // returns " DISTINCT WHERE status = 'active'"

methods.javadoc_summary: Sets or replaces the WHERE clause.
contract: If a WHERE clause already exists, it will be replaced.
examples:
Criteria c = Criteria.builder()
    .where(Filters.and(Filters.equal("status", "active"), Filters.greaterThan("age", 18)))
    .build();
c.toSql(NamingPolicy.NO_CHANGE);   // returns " WHERE ((status = 'active') AND (age > 18))"

// Edge cases:
Criteria.builder().where((Condition) null);   // throws IllegalArgumentException
Criteria.builder().where(new GroupBy("x"));   // throws IllegalArgumentException (wrong clause)

types.name: Operator
types.javadoc_summary: Enumeration of SQL operators supported by the condition framework.

methods.name: of
methods.signature: public static Operator of(final String name)

params.name: name
params.javadoc: the SQL token or enum constant name. May be null; surrounding whitespace is not trimmed.

methods.javadoc_summary: Returns an Operator by its SQL representation.
examples:
// Look up symbolic operators
Operator eq = Operator.of("=");       // Operator.EQUAL
Operator gt = Operator.of(">");       // Operator.GREATER_THAN
Operator gte = Operator.of(">=");     // Operator.GREATER_THAN_OR_EQUAL

// Look up word operators (case-insensitive)
Operator and = Operator.of("AND");    // Operator.AND
Operator or = Operator.of("or");      // Operator.OR
Operator like = Operator.of("LIKE");  // Operator.LIKE

// Enum constant names are also accepted (case-insensitive)
Operator eq2 = Operator.of("EQUAL");          // Operator.EQUAL
Operator gt2 = Operator.of("greater_than");    // Operator.GREATER_THAN

// Edge cases
Operator unknown = Operator.of("UNKNOWN");    // null (not a known token or name)
Operator empty = Operator.of("");             // Operator.EMPTY (its SQL token is "")
Operator nil = Operator.of(null);             // null

methods.name: sqlToken
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the SQL representation of this operator.
examples:
String eqToken = Operator.EQUAL.sqlToken();           // "="
String andToken = Operator.AND.sqlToken();            // "AND"
String betweenToken = Operator.BETWEEN.sqlToken();    // "BETWEEN"
String likeToken = Operator.LIKE.sqlToken();          // "LIKE"
String joinToken = Operator.LEFT_JOIN.sqlToken();     // "LEFT JOIN"
String emptyToken = Operator.EMPTY.sqlToken();        // "" (empty placeholder operator)

methods.name: toString
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the SQL representation of this operator.
examples:
String eq = Operator.EQUAL.toString();                  // "="
String and = Operator.AND.toString();                   // "AND"
String gte = Operator.GREATER_THAN_OR_EQUAL.toString(); // ">="
String join = Operator.LEFT_JOIN.toString();            // "LEFT JOIN"
String empty = Operator.EMPTY.toString();               // "" (empty placeholder operator)

// toString() always equals sqlToken()
boolean same = Operator.IN.toString().equals(Operator.IN.sqlToken()); // true

methods.name: renderValue
methods.signature: public static String renderValue(final Object value)

params.name: value
params.javadoc: the value to render

methods.javadoc_summary: Converts a value to its SQL representation.
contract: This method performs SQL escaping and formatting: null values become the string "null" Strings are wrapped in single quotes and escaped via AbstractCondition#escapeStringLiteral(String): embedded unescaped single and double quotes are backslash-escaped (' becomes \', " becomes \"); a backslash shields the character that follows it, so any existing \x pair \u2014 including an already-escaped quote such as \' \u2014 is copied verbatim rather than escaped again, plus a defensive guard that appends one extra backslash when the body would otherwise end in an unescaped trailing backslash Number values must render as decimal, integer, or scientific-notation literals; NaN/infinite Float/Double values and non-numeric custom text are rejected.

SqlExpression objects return their literal SQL text (or "null" if the literal is null) SubQuery instances render their toString() wrapped in parentheses; other Conditions use their toString() verbatim Other objects are converted via N#stringOf(Object), then quoted and escaped Usage Examples:
examples:
SqlExpression.renderValue("text");                      // returns "'text'"
SqlExpression.renderValue("O'Brien");                   // returns "'O\'Brien'" (single quote backslash-escaped)
SqlExpression.renderValue("say \"hi\"");                // returns "'say \"hi\"'" (double quote backslash-escaped)
SqlExpression.renderValue(123);                         // returns "123"
SqlExpression.renderValue(45.67);                       // returns "45.67"
SqlExpression.renderValue(null);                        // returns "null"
SqlExpression.renderValue(true);                        // returns "true"
SqlExpression.renderValue(false);                       // returns "false"
SqlExpression.renderValue(new SqlExpression("COUNT(*)"));  // returns "COUNT(*)" (the expression's literal)
SqlExpression.renderValue(Double.NaN);                  // throws IllegalArgumentException

methods.name: toSql

params.name: namingPolicy
params.javadoc: the naming policy to apply to detected identifiers; if null, NamingPolicy#NO_CHANGE is used

methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the string form of this expression, with the naming policy applied to any identifiers (column or property names) that can be detected within the literal.
contract: Recognized SQL keyword tokens are also left unchanged when written in their canonical upper-case form (for example CURRENT_DATE); a lower-case token is treated as an identifier and converted.
examples:
SqlExpression.of("firstName").toSql(NamingPolicy.SNAKE_CASE);          // returns "first_name"
SqlExpression.of("_firstName").toSql(NamingPolicy.SNAKE_CASE);         // returns "_first_name"
SqlExpression.of("firstName = 'John'").toSql(NamingPolicy.SNAKE_CASE); // returns "first_name = 'John'" (identifier converted, quoted literal kept)
SqlExpression.of("price-tax").toSql(NamingPolicy.CAMEL_CASE);          // returns "price-tax" (SQL subtraction preserved; each operand converted independently)
SqlExpression.of("price  *  2").toSql(NamingPolicy.NO_CHANGE);         // returns "price * 2" (parser path collapses whitespace runs)
SqlExpression.of("firstName").toSql(NamingPolicy.NO_CHANGE);           // returns "firstName"
SqlExpression.of("firstName").toSql(null);                             // returns "firstName" (null defaults to NO_CHANGE)
SqlExpression.of("").toSql(NamingPolicy.NO_CHANGE);                    // returns "" (empty literal)
// an uninitialized instance (null literal, only possible via deserialization) returns "null"

methods.name: hashCode
methods.javadoc_summary: Computes the hash code based on the literal string.
examples:
new SqlExpression("price * quantity").hashCode();                            // returns "price * quantity".hashCode()
SqlExpression.of("a + b").hashCode() == SqlExpression.of("a + b").hashCode();   // true (same literal)

methods.name: equals

params.name: obj
params.javadoc: the object to compare with

methods.javadoc_summary: Checks if this expression equals another object.
contract: Checks if this expression equals another object.
Two expressions are equal if they are both SqlExpression instances with the same literal string.
examples:
new SqlExpression("a + b").equals(new SqlExpression("a + b"));   // returns true (same literal)
SqlExpression.of("a + b").equals(SqlExpression.of("a + b"));     // returns true (cached, same instance)
new SqlExpression("a + b").equals(new SqlExpression("a - b"));   // returns false (different literal)
new SqlExpression("a + b").equals("a + b");                   // returns false (not an SqlExpression)

methods.javadoc_summary: Checks if this subquery is equal to another object.
contract: Checks if this subquery is equal to another object.
Two subqueries are equal only when all of their identity fields are equal: the entity name, the entity class, the selected properties, the raw SQL string, and the condition.

types.javadoc_summary: Represents a USING clause in SQL JOIN operations.

constructors.javadoc_summary: Creates a USING clause with the specified column names.

deprecated.message: It's recommended to use Filters#on(java.util.Map) instead of Using for better portability and clarity. Replace new Using("col1", "col2") with explicit Filters.on(N.asMap("table1.col1", "table2.col1", "table1.col2", "table2.col2")).

params.name: columnNames
params.javadoc: variable number of column names to join on. All columns must exist in both tables with identical names. Must not be null or empty, and individual names must not be null, empty, or blank. Names must be unqualified (cannot contain a .) and must each be a single column name (cannot contain ,, (, or )).

constructors.javadoc_summary: Creates a USING clause with a collection of column names.

deprecated.message: It's recommended to use Filters#on(java.util.Map) instead of Using for better portability and clarity. Replace new Using(columnList) with an explicit Filters.on(Map) condition that specifies the full column names with table prefixes.

params.name: columnNames
params.javadoc: collection of column names to join on. Must not be null or empty, and individual names must not be null, empty, or blank. Names must be unqualified (cannot contain a .) and must each be a single column name (cannot contain ,, (, or )). The collection is read once and snapshotted. Order matters for some databases; use a LinkedHashSet or List to preserve insertion order.

methods.name: columnNames
methods.javadoc_summary: Returns the validated column names this USING clause joins on, in the order they were supplied.
